Wait, this scaley cape... are these guards just for the Shrine of Nurtury?
There are too many of them... not sure how to get past. They've blocked off all the paths.
What do we do?
We're here.
Is this the place where Extra's resting?
Yes. We can arrange for visits, if you so desire.
Dedicated Nurturers have been assigned to her. She will receive the best possible care until she gives birth.
What about us?
Thirteen nomadic abbeys, with patients in the four figures. The Pagus Ambrosius you put us in doesn't have that many hospitals or sanatoriums...
The Summit of Nations, with all its attendees, has stretched Laterano's medical resources to its limits.
So...
Taking in a pregnant woman already represents exceptional circumstances.
However, you have come here out of trust in Laterano, and Laterano must repay that trust.
The Shrine of Nurtury is not scheduled to open to the public until after the Nativity. Most of the rooms are unoccupied at this time, and our Nurturers have received basic medical training.
The Matriarch has already discussed this with the Curia. The Shrine of Nurtury will take in our friends from outside on a temporary basis, until more permanent arrangements can be made.
The guards will escort you to your rooms. I must remain here, as more patients are expected to arrive.
The Lord's blessing be upon Laterano, and upon all.
See, there's a way.
Don't tell me—
Salve, Cardinal Velliv, it's been a while.
Not 'cardinal' anymore. I'm just an ordinary clerk in the Shrine of Nurtury now.
Just 'Velliv', then? Anyway, it seems like your fingerprints are all over it.
What do you mean?
The security measures I saw on my way in from the streets. They could go toe-to-toe with the Pontifica Cohors.
I have to earn my keep, after all.
Anyway, it looks like I guessed right.
What?
It was indeed you who came to review our security... even though this is a job usually reserved for Notarial Hall executors, or the gun-knights and Pontifica Cohors.
I'm still investigating the bombing that happened a couple of days ago. I'm not exactly an outsider.
I wasn't instructed to take the bomb investigation into account when planning security. Besides, your investigation hasn't exactly turned up anything concrete, has it?
Unless you mean to tell me you came because it was Andoain who was attacked?
Are you going to chide me that I should be thinking of the whole Holy City as a cardinal of the Seventh Tribunal, and not about my personal relationships?
We know just what kind of person you are.
Sharp, astute, decisive, all traits that served you well at the consistory...
But we also know when these virtues of yours are most prominently displayed.
I remember the reasons why you joined the Pontifica Cohors commandos, Cardinal Lemuen. I read that report.
Hah. Do you really? I've forgotten.
You didn't really have any interest in the commandos. You just went to see Fiammetta, didn't you?
And Andoain.
Really? That I didn't know. I thought you met after you joined the commandos.
We met before that.
It seemed like he had come from a far away place, walking on foot. His clothes were tattered, and he himself was just as ragged.
He sat on a bench in front of the chapel, still and unmoving, looking more like a statue than the actual statues of the saints on the roadside.
That was the first time Mostima and I saw him.
We met Fiammetta at the midnight movies. We hit it off quickly.
Andoain had trouble fitting in, having grown up outside the Holy City. Fiammetta, stubborn girl that she is, was in another one of those fights with her grandpa... Mostima and I wanted to see them.
They didn't know each other beforehand, and the other Sanktas probably didn't intend to isolate them, but either way, the end result was—
The two of them were the only ones left behind in the rubber-bullet roulette that was the commandos' initiation.
And so you beat up everyone else on the scene...
You and Mostima filled out your applications right there, becoming the last four-man squad at that initiation.
You ended up being the most active and most coordinated squad on the force, until the incident...
There is a lot I admire about you, Lemuen, but I must remind you... as a cardinal, the Holy City must be your first, foremost, and only concern.
To me, the Holy City begins with a long list of familiar names.
So much for reminiscing.
The Seventh Tribunal knows our duty. If you really think of yourself as an ordinary clerk, then maybe I should pay a visit to the Matriarch personally.
She's right up ahead, isn't she?
Lemuen spots the one she came to see.
The golden-haired Sankta stands under a porch. She is praying, anxious; her breathing is rapid, and she trembles visibly...
Lemuen can feel herself overwhelmed by her counterpart's raging emotions.
Matriarch?
I overheard your conversation with Velliv, Cardinal Lemuen... pardon my inability to converse calmly at this time.
What's in there?
A Sarkaz mother.
She spent much time in the wilderness, and declined food for two days after she arrived... she is not in good shape. Our Nurturers are doing their best.
She suffers from fear and anxiety. She does not want to give in to the pain, and continues her struggle to stay awake...
She is praying, awake, praying that her child will be born safely.
All I can do is empathize and pray with her.
You can empathize with a Sarkaz?
Yes, Cardinal.
As you worry for my outpouring of emotion. It is as clear as the innate empathy between you and I.
'Empathy'...
A blessing that the Lord has bestowed upon me, perhaps, in place of my sight... or perhaps simply because this place was built by Laterano to welcome new life.
Here our faith is strengthened and expanded. Here, there are no barriers, no limits.
Putting it in that light, it is our own people who need this place the most.
Sunlight shines through the stained glass, illuminating the empty and solemn hall.
The massive statue stands with its palms together, looking down on the two tiny Sanktas beneath it.
It's certainly a special place.
I've seen plenty of statues in all sorts of poses, holding a gun and ready to shoot. But it's the first time I've seen one like this, and it takes some getting used to.
Compared to the so-called 'Lateran statues' that line the hills of Bolívar or the shores of Iberia, the statues seen in Laterano are the more incomprehensible, Cardinal.
Paradise has shaped our faith, so peculiar that it has become somewhat distorted.
The souls of the living praying for survival, as they traverse Catastrophe and suffering... such is the most basic face of faith.
M-Matriarch. Blessings to you.
The mother's condition has stabilized. We have given her medicine and nutrition. She is sleeping now.
That is comforting to hear.
She is expecting soon. Please keep a close eye on her, and do not let yourselves grow complacent.
Yes, Matriarch.
Praise be to the Lord.
I can feel the relief and joy coming from the bottom of your heart.
Thank you for your companionship, Cardinal. I hope empathy did not cause my anxiety to affect you.
I'm not so weak. Besides, your words were inspiring, to an extent.
You mentioned that you only came here later?
Yes.
Long had I wandered the wilderness, my companions withered in the mud...
In order to find Laterano?
No, at the time I never imagined that there would be such a city upon this land.
But our shared vision finally helped me locate it.
Our encounter here and now was written in fate.
It is a blessing to be able to serve Laterano and my kindred, after all this time, frail as I may be.
I have a friend who works here. He also came from afar...
In Laterano today and Laterano tomorrow, everyone must and will find their own place and play their own roles... only then can we hope to fend off calamity.
It seems we've gotten sidetracked.
If there has been an attack on the Nurturers, the Seventh Tribunal is happy to work with the Shrine of Nurtury in implementing security measures, to ensure that the Nativity goes off without a hitch.
I understand your worry for your friend, and your curiosity about this shrine.
As I said, though, we must all find our own place and play our own roles.
And there I was, thinking our chat was going nicely.
Looks like I've been summarily rejected, haven't I?
...I'm sorry for your loss.
I should have brought a few wheat stalks, if I only knew this had happened.
Wheat stalks?
For funerals at the Landen Monastery, we weave a laurel out of wheat stalks and place it upon the departed's coffin.
That way, the soul of the departed will be comforted by the scent of bread and beer on the other side.
Thank you, kind sister.
My brother and I were exiled by the General Chamber of Commerce because we were afflicted with Oripathy, and did not have the skill to participate in knightsports.
We came all this way to Laterano, only for his condition to worsen and take his life...
But the Curia helped bury him, allowing him to die with dignity... there was even a passing Sankta who played a requiem for him.
I see...
Was that Sankta a female with long, straight black hair, playing a cello with an unusual shape?
Er, yes.
She played for you?
Were you subject to any hallucinations, or any psychological discomfort?
What do mean by discomfort? I know it made me cry. It was my brother, after all...
Senior Executor, I thought we agreed that I would do the questioning...
Other executors from Notarial Hall will soon be here to take these refugees to Pagus Ambrosius. We do not have much time.
They are not suspected of any crime. I did not find complex interrogation techniques to be necessary.
I visited Lungmen and Victoria when I was promoting Landen Monastery, and I saw many people like them.
They were forced to leave home due to unfortunate circumstances. Their previous dealings with the police have made them...
Executors are more or less police, right?
Uh, there's no need. You guys just took in outsiders with Oripathy like us, even though I heard you used to kick your own Infected out.
So, right now, I don't have anything against the Lateran government. Besides, this gentleman here looks like a decent fellow...
Is there something wrong with the black-haired Sankta you were asking about?
Without permission, she left the monastery where she was under observation. We have tracked her here.
Sh-She's a prisoner? I thought she was an official from your, what's that, Sixth Tribunal?
Why did you think that?
She asked us about the abbey after the funeral, whether there was any equipment in disrepair, any safety hazards, any supplies we were short on.
She made it a point to ask if anyone who recently entered Laterano had disappeared, especially any pregnant women...
(Doesn't sound like Arturia just happened to pass by...)
How did it go?
You know the answer.
The Matriarch's response was just like yours—'don't poke your head into other people's business.'
Hehe.
I know you have good intentions, Lemuen, but you're also showing that you don't trust me.
I beg your understanding.
The Shrine of Nurtury, this great structure that casts a shadow over Laterano, which no one's quite sure when it came to be...
You, Reverend Patrizion and the Matriarch promise that this will be the birthplace of the future. Even the suspect behind the bombings seems interested in it.
How can the Seventh Tribunal, responsible for intelligence and national security, ignore something so prominent?
Of course, I understand.
Excuse me.
The Nurturer leads a group past. Their footsteps echo through the empty hall.
The people in the procession come from many different races. None of them speak, but all glance up and down at this unfamiliar space.
Lemuen steps aside to let them pass.
What is it, Cardinal Lemuen?
Nothing.
What I mean is, curiosity killed the Feline. I guess I should expect a complaint from my predecessor if I continue to insist.
Why are you leaning against the wall in a weird pose? I thought I was looking at a statue.
You could have heard my joints crack if you came in earlier.
I should pay a visit to an artificer, see if I can't get some cushioning for my waist.
I'm not sure the gun-knight armorsmiths take into account the special needs of the elderly.
Young gun-knights need it too! It helps mitigate the recoil from our guns!
The Nativity is tomorrow. How are preparations at the Shrine of Nurtury?
The mother is in stable condition. Andoain has taken good care of her. She will give birth to a healthy, beautiful infant tomorrow.

Wiš'adel and the Kazdelian delegation, the Twin Empresses and the Leithanian delegation, the delegation from the Ægir Institute of Development Planning, the Tianshi delegation led by Yan's Envoy of Ceremonial Rites...
Tomorrow, all the visitors to the Holy City are invited to the Shrine of Nurtury to witness the birth of a new life.
Why did you need me, then, if everything is in order?
Our newest cardinal...
Lemuen?
She came to see the Matriarch. I'm wondering—
Did she 'happen' to pass by as she was investigating the bombings, 'drop in' to take a look, and then propose to provide help to the Shrine of Nurtury on a 'whim'?
At first, I thought she came because of the attack on Andoain. But when I saw the look on her face after she saw the Matriarch... it is clear that she has suspicions.
I thought I quelled those.
Lemuen possesses a keen edge that few others have, especially when focused on a single matter. There is certainly no better fit for a cardinal of the Seventh Tribunal.
At the same time, there is also no worse fit. More often than not, she relies solely on her own judgment when it comes to where she will focus her attention.
Do we need to be on guard against her?
You make us sound like some evil organization plotting to overthrow Laterano.
Who is to say?
For some, the future that we usher in will overturn life as they know it.
Our empathy, our kindred and family, even the concept of 'Sankta' itself... existing limitations will be cast aside.
This should have been the predetermined end of Laterano's path, but the path has gone on far too long.
Some people take the accidents that happened in the process as the norm, and now they can't accept the predetermined end.
That is correct.
We should prepare early.
I'll deal with Lemuen. You head back to the Shrine of Nurtury and get ready for tomorrow's ceremony.
May the Lord's blessings be with this city.
The red-headed Sankta runs through a forest of white and blue, like a tiny flame flickering in the rain.
That cloud on that comfort bell is almost the size of Croissant's van... and a cradle large enough to hold two cargo containers...
The clouds hanging upon the comfort bells seem to block out the sunlight, and the hands holding up the cradles reaching towards the clouds.
In this place, every living thing appears small and insignificant.
Huh?
What the heck...?
It feels so empty and quiet, but more than half of these nurseries are occupied...
Children, outsiders, even Fallen Angels... the Nurturers are busy. Looks like they've been living here for a while.
Didn't they say most of the facilities are empty and will only open to the public when that ceremony is done?
That's not true at all!
I have to recover the bombs!
I thought I could stop the ceremony by blowing up the venue, but this place is full of people...
To think I went through all that effort to slip past the guards...
...I wish the girls were here.
Texas could've knocked out all the guards and dragged them outside, or Croissant could've barged into the rooms and packed up everyone inside...
Or I could just get the boss to do a meet-and-greet opposite the ceremony to draw everybody's attention...
Ugh, forget it. There's no time, I gotta get to work...
I'll take these bombs apart and think of another way—
It'll be easier if you cut the blue wire first.
Whah...?
When did you get back?
El.
An entirely unremarkable apartment, except for the fact that its roof offers a perfect view of the plaza in front of the Shrine of Nurtury.
Is that why you chose to hide here, Arturia?
Another happy reunion.
And much sooner than I expected.
You did not attempt to conceal your trail.
The paper dragonfly was a clear clue. I saw you use such musical Arts more than once in Zwillingstürme.
Well, I hate to admit it...
But you've learned a whole lot of my secrets, Fede.
Wait, you...?
Did our executor here not tell you that I am his beloved soror, Sister Hildegard?
Huh?
You are my distant relative. And that does not impede me from exercising oversight over you.
Before I brought you back to Laterano, on the outskirts of Zwillingstürme, I warned you—
I will increase my vigilance over you in order to determine the subjective motivations and possible objective consequences of your actions.
And I will shoot to kill if I find anything suspicious.
I remember the sunset that day.
So hardheaded he's fun, isn't he, Sister Hildegard?
You were always cooperative, Arturia. That's why Landen Monastery gave you a degree of leeway.
You deceived us!
I apologize, Sister.
I hope my impertinence will not be detrimental to you or to Landen Monastery.
Why did you leave Landen Monastery without permission, Arturia?
Look at this shrine, Fede.
So vast, so full of vitality. It reaches into the clouds, surpassing even the eponymous twin towers of Zwillingstürme in Leithanien...
Is this the 'grace' and 'authority' that Laterano wants to build?
Look at the haloless outsiders being guided inside.
What are Laterano's plans for them?
What have you found, Arturia?
Do you remember our short stay at Rhodes Island before returning to Laterano?
I met a mysterious guest there, who told me that there may come a day when Laterano is no longer a warm, cozy little cottage for the Sanktas.
Fede, Sister Hildegard. Look at what lies before you. Do you not suspect that the day has come?
What has become of the Holy City that we were so proud of?
Wh-What do you mean?
You are ever vigilant, Fede. You must have noticed something, no?
Otherwise, you would have greeted me with the barrel of your gun.
Are those bombs?
They look pretty powerful.
Sis! You're walking again?!
Uh-huh.
Hooray!
Was it you that I saw on Via Jovita, and that Amos saw outside the firearms workshop?
How did you manage to make so many bombs despite the Curia's strict control of explosive materials...? You're as good at this as ever.
Hehe.
Alright. Spit it out, El. What are you trying to do?
Blow up the shrine?
Stop the Nativity?
Or sabotage the whole Summit of Nations?
Stay back, sis.
A massive pillar cuts the light and shadow open. Lemuen stops in her tracks, keeping her distance from Exusiai.
That's a cardinal's cassock you're wearing, isn't it?
Does it look good on me?
Totally! It's so cool!
It was a little loose around the waist when I got it the day before my creation. I had to stay up all night fixing it.
Ugh, I wish I wasn't wearing this for our reunion.
Did you come back by yourself, or do you have friends with you?
Hah, you're pumping me for information now?
You're a simple girl, after all. You're not in a school, and this isn't a graduation ceremony. Did you really just decide to liven things up like this on your own?
Who's behind you, and why did they put you up to this?
Hey, let's try this—'Apple pie with mayonnaise!'
'Had a slice, want another.'
You remembered!
When we had a fight that wasn't going anywhere or a secret we didn't want to share, that's our password to set things aside.
I can feel your emotions, El. Agitation, impatience, worry, not even knowing how to explain...
But my job is to capture the culprits who are trying to sabotage the Summit of Nations. I will file a request with the Curia to personally handle the follow-up to this case.
The Sankta sisters stand off against one another, neither making a single move, nor speaking a single word. It is Lemuen who finally breaks the silence with a sigh.
*sigh*...
In that instant, Exusiai flies toward her and hugs her tightly.
Now that's the reaction I know from an El in trouble.
I missed you, sis!
It's been a while. Have you grown?
Of course not! I was almost an adult when I left Laterano!
You don't have to talk like you're my mom, sis.
Is that what I sound like?
Did you tie your hair yourself? It's coming loose.
Let me tie it for you... still careless as ever.
Of course, I'm not as good as you at that.
What are you doing here, anyway? Don't tell me you knew I would show up?
Just reviewing the shrine's security measures... I thought it was remarkable enough to see the Matriarch praying for a pregnant Sarkaz...
And then I ran into you.
(Matriarch... pregnant Sarkaz... praying...?)
(Could the Nativity be...?)
So you don't know what's in this shrine either?
Oh, is it your turn to pump me for information?
Who taught you to do that?
I didn't turn bad, I promise! The people who're supporting me now—they're a good influence, I promise!
I'm not alone, and I'm not being used.
There's a lot that's wrong here, but it's hard to explain... I need more time.
I'll come home after I've done what I have to!
Lemuen ties one last knot with the string, then pushes up slightly so the strand of hair on the side of her sister's head will not come loose so easily.
The next instant, she finds that her arms are empty.
I'll leave the disarming of the bombs to you, sis—I mean, Your Eminence!
Lemuen hears the ticking of bomb countdowns in all directions. She looks in the direction where her sister disappeared, but is in no position to give chase.
She never changes, does she?
Citizen, if you are here to view the Nativity, please queue up at the security check line in the plaza.
You've been dawdling here for a while.
Oh, I'm sorry!
I'm just waiting for someone...
Oh?
There is a large amount of traffic going in and out of the shrine today. We ask for your cooperation and to refrain from loitering.
S-Sorry.
El's been in there all day.
The plan was to blow up important structural elements in the shrine to prevent the ceremony... whatever it's called... from being held as scheduled.
She should have been out by now... don't tell me she's been caught?
But I've been here all this time, and there's been no movement inside...
But El must have ran into some kind of trouble that's holding her up...
Come on, Vani, think... what can you do?
I have two small Originium powder tubes that I could turn into an improvised bomb.
It won't be very powerful, but it should cause enough commotion to distract the guards and allow El to...
Let's set it here.
No one's paying any attention... all I need to do is set it off—
Vannini's arm finds the barrel of a gun, preventing it from moving down any further.
Don't let that thing go boom, girl.
Stand up, slowly.
R-Reverend Gun-knight...
Those tools and all, you work at a firearms workshop?
I have ten minutes. Explain yourself, girl.
What are you up to here? Or should I say, who are you trying to cover for?
Let's go, El.
What about Master Pag?
Magister's sleeping. His vision's been getting worse, and the doctor wants him to get plenty of rest.
We shouldn't wake him if we're careful.
There's something I need to tell you first, Vani.
It's so weird when you talk all serious like that...
Ahem. Listen—
If you get caught, just give me up to whoever it is, whether it's the Pontifica Cohors, the executors, or the gun-knights. Don't worry about me.
I came in the middle of the night, kicked your door open, and forced you to make bombs and stand guard for me.
You're an innocent little angel who doesn't know anything!
Wha?
That's my condition for letting you come with me.
Just because I'm shy doesn't mean I'm a coward!
Haha. I know. And I'm glad you decided to help me.
The Curia seems to be taking that ceremony very seriously. Who knows what punishment they have in store for somebody trying to bomb it?
Besides, Laterano is very different from how I remember it.
But...
No but's or what about's! I can handle this on my own.
Besides, Penguin Logistics company culture says, never share the glory when you can hoard it all for yourself!
(Shakes head)
I-I came on my own.
Interesting girl.
Old men like me don't get a lot of chances to talk to the youth. Unfortunately, the ceremony is about to begin...
If you won't talk, I'll have to hand you over to the executors of the Notarial Hall.
Stop right there, Patrizion!
Don't think I don't recognize that sanctimonious face of yours just because you're hiding it behind a hunk of sheet metal!
*pant* *pant* My... my legs...
Pag?
What do you mean? Is something going on with the Holy City? Would you care to explain, or do I have to guess from your meaningful glances at one another?
This is not related to our investigation, Sister.
At this time, I have not determined that you need to know more.
In any case, Arturia, you'll have to come back with us.
I don't dislike you. In fact, I am rather fond of you. But if your actions will bring harm to Landen Monastery, then I-I'll pull no punches!
May I ask you something, Sister Hildegard?
Huh?
You love the monastery and Laterano, don't you?
Or should I say—how did you establish an emotional connection to Laterano as a Liberi?

Well... I've lived at Landen Monastery for a long time. I was brought up by the brothers and sisters there, and we worked together to make bread and beer, doing our best to keep the monastery solvent.
I belong here. Of course I love this place.
If you're asking why there are so many Liberis in the Holy City... you've read The Saints' Travels, haven't you?
Don't tell me you only used it to make your paper dragonfly...?
According to The Saints' Travels, the first encounter between the Liberi and Laterano began with a herd of burdenbeasts.

Many years ago, a group of Liberi were scattered by a herd of burdenbeasts while seeking water in the wilderness. The animals carried grain on their backs, forming an orderly procession as they traveled into the distance.
The Liberi spent great effort to hunt the burdenbeasts. They never imagined that these creatures of the wild could move in such orderly manner, so they followed the beasts.
Then, the Liberi saw the halo-bearers in the fields, singing as they guided the herd of burdenbeasts into the assembly of pearl-white buildings behind them.
At a time when the Liberi still lived naked in the wild, seeking water and game, those people had learned to tame wild beasts, and build cities safe from Catastrophe.
More importantly, the Liberi found that those people had a confidence and optimism that was alien to the Liberi.
Because of their halos, and 'empathy'...
Uh-huh.
From the moment of their birth, they could feel each other's feelings and understand one another. They rarely knew conflict, and they were able to overcome crises that would destroy any other race.
They created the perfect order, naming it the Law.
I can understand that feeling.
Even as the Curia cut off funding to the monastery, even as its buildings fell into disrepair, even as I fretted over the monastery's books...
The Sanktas of Landen Monastery never seemed to fear that there might come a day when we would have to close our doors.
Their confidence and optimism let them accept the presence of the Liberi nearby, learning from them and becoming part of the whole.
Yes. Over the ages, the Sanktas have almost completely assimilated the Holy City's Liberi... other than the halo, which can never be shared.
We are too special, able to take in everything... like a concerto with a clear main theme.
Laterano has never been just a warm, cozy little cottage for the Sanktas, Fede.
After you escaped, you went searching for a 'missing pregnant woman' from a nomadic abbey, but there have been no such recent reports of a missing person in Notarial Hall records.
Explain yourself, Arturia.
A poor mother has gone missing in Laterano. A little incident that no one has paid any heed...
But, by chance, I had once touched the note of her heart while playing in the wheat fields—
And I discovered where she went.
Do you mean...?
Patience, Fede. Wait and see.
We've come this far. You don't need me to explain it to you.
Is this the girl you took in?
You'll have to forgive, Reverend Gun-knight, that I don't have the adoption papers on me.
What? If you can take in a Liberi, why can't I take in a Sankta?
She's a good girl. You know, you are the only Sankta I've ever had a real problem understanding.
That mouth of yours...
How long has it been since we last met, Pag?
I forget. Never thought much about it. Didn't think I would need to.
Back on topic: Your apprentice just attempted to—
On my orders.
Why?
An act of protest. Is that so hard to understand?
The Curia shut down over a hundred firearms workshops overnight. My livelihood for decades, the shop I counted on for my retirement, all gone in the blink of an eye.
When I heard you were throwing some party here, I thought I might force the Curia to hear the voice of this humble citizen, in front of all these honored guests.
I should have done it myself, but I'm old, and my legs and eyes aren't what they used to be, so I had the girl run this errand for me.
I thought you had gone into a different line of work, Pag.
Screw that.
Why should I? When you still have the nerve to use that gun I made after you exiled Gia from Laterano?
I just gave up being a dedicated supplier for you gun-knights.
True.
And how exactly do you plan on dealing with this?
We're caught red-handed attempting to bomb the Shrine of Nurtury and sabotage the Nativity.
Will you once again 'escort' your friend out of Laterano, as a gun-knight sworn to protect the sanctity of Paradise?
One who just happens to be a Liberi as well.
This has nothing to do with you being Liberi, Pag. If you have indeed violated the Law, then I have no choice but to suffer another parting.
And yet... I see that no harm was actually done, was there?
Take your apprentice and go.
Hmph.
Come along, Vani.
(Grab onto me. I left in a hurry and forgot the new glasses you got me.)
Reverend, the man's story doesn't stand up to scrutiny.
I know.
The appropriate course of action would be to take them in for questioning and eliminate any potential risk...
That won't be necessary... besides, I don't need another fight with that old bloke.
This bomb is simple, improvised. The girl obviously made it on the spot... which means that her accomplice is still inside.
Reassign an extra team of gun-knights and work with the patrols to ensure the shrine's security.
Yes, Reverend.
Strange. Is she still in the Shrine of Nurtury?
Cardinal Aide Amos.
R-Reverend Patrizion. L-Long time no see.
Long time indeed.
What are you all worked up about?
Have you seen Cardinal Lemuen? There's something I wanted to check with her.
She hasn't returned to the Seventh Tribunal since visiting the Shrine of Nurtury.
If this has something to do with the explosion, I must be kept in the loop.
Yes, Reverend.
I bumped into a Sankta on Via Borgos in Pagus Faber to complete the takeover of a firearms workshop. There was a visible reaction from the Cardinal when I mentioned this to her.
I checked the appearance of the individual I encountered against the database of registered citizens, and found a match in the external personnel files.
She must have been on the scene of the explosion in Pagus Stevonus. She has... certain ties to the Cardinal.
Oh?
...I don't have much time, Sister. I must return to the Shrine of Nurtury.
Let me confirm: You're not sure if the individual currently under care will die, but you want to make funeral preparations for her?
Yes... she is not in good shape. I will do all that I can as her Nurturer.
But that will not preclude me from preparing for the worst.
This seems so... un-Lateran.
What should a Lateran be like?
Now that's a complicated question...
Farewell—I hope we will not have cause to meet again.
I didn't believe it when they said it—But you really did come back here, Andoain.
This Ecclesia Requietum, this field of irises... are they familiar to you? Do you reminisce over them?
There are many Ecclesiae Requietorum in Laterano.
As for reminiscing... it would be inappropriate for me to do that about an Ecclesia Requietum, in my present position.
I forgot. You're a high-and-mighty Nurturer now.
Who are you?
Was it you who followed me and attacked me in the shrine?
Followed? Attacked? Who's the one who's mad here, Andoain, you or me?
If you didn't want us to walk the same path as you, why did you call us 'Pathfinders'?
The sinister shrine that devours. Is it the righteous path that you've found, or one that has led you astray?
I do not recall promising to walk the same path as anyone, before I met the Matriarch...
As for the Shrine of Nurtury, it's true that it has guided my path. But sinister? There must be some misunderstanding...
Enough, Andoain! Nugas garris! Don't play the fool with me!
A dozen of us never came back from the shrine! If the path you found demands our sacrifice, we at least deserve to be told.
Or, if you're really shameless enough to deny the connection, at least have the decency to say it to my face!
No, I don't understand...
You'll have plenty of time to figure it out.
Please, don't... there are many children I must care for... and that mother...
*cough* *cough* Argh—dammit!
It's them. Pull back!
If I didn't know better, I would say you were trying to hit Andoain with that one.
He's proven hard enough to kill so far. I figured it was an acceptable risk.
Fiammetta... Mostima...
You've really made yourself a lot of enemies, haven't you, Andoain?
I'll go after them while you two talk.
There's no need.
I don't know why they're targeting me, but I can't help but feel...
They're determined. People like that inevitably have complex pasts and simple motivations.
How compassionate.
How could you do what you did to your own cohort, then?
Why don't you repent before Mostima and Lemuen?
Why wouldn't you tell us what the one in the Lock and Key made you see, deep in that dungeon?
Why didn't you ever give us an answer?
Or, more accurately, share the question with us.
We must each follow our own paths, Fiammetta.
Is that why you attacked Lemuen and fled, before coming back with that Matriarch, hiding in the Shrine of Nurtury under her protection like nothing ever happened? Is this your 'path'?
What 'salvation' did you find in the years that you went off the grid?
I never found salvation, nor do I feel like nothing ever happened.
However, life is short, and if I do not find the answer I seek soon, my sins and your pain will become meaningless.
The day after I was released from Landen Monastery, I left Laterano, believing that I would find something.
The next five years were like a blur. All I remember is going from one place to another, alone, in many directions without end...
I often wonder if something has gone wrong with my memory.
And then, I found the Matriarch.
She was headed for the Holy City, though she had been delayed greatly by helping the wounded and needy along the way.
She made me believe that the answer still lies in Laterano.
She promised me a miracle, and that thousands upon thousands more miracles will be born from us. I returned to Laterano to fulfill this promise with her.
Taceas! Just shut up! You're babbling to yourself and making zero sense.
Listen here, Andoain.
After I kicked you out of that gathering the other day, Patrizion spent a long time telling me how important you, that shrine of yours, and the ceremony you're holding are.
I've never seen the old man so serious. So I promised him I wouldn't pick a fight with you. Yet.
I didn't save your life because I've forgiven you.
You just don't deserve to die before you've truly repented for what you did and paid a proper price.
The Nativity is about to start, isn't it?
I'm sorry, I must go.
Good morning.
Honored kindred, returned Messengers, emissaries from all across Terra, and guests who have set foot in the Holy City for the first time through the abbeys, I greet you.
This shrine in which we now find ourselves was not built to demonstrate Laterano's power and authority for the Summit of Nations.
However, I am glad from the bottom of my heart that it was completed in time for all of us to gather here today.
Every birth brings new possibilities... for the continuation of faith, for the fight against calamity. Only in this way can we shape the future.
This is even more true for the Laterano of the future.
I bid you now, prepare, and listen to the heartbeat of new life.
Wait a moment, Senior Executor. So you never intended to bring Arturia back to Landen Monastery?
I never stated that I would.
Well, if you won't, I will!
There are certain things I must still ascertain with Arturia.
Now, now, Federico, it won't do to torment a poor nun like that.
Richele?
Did you have to run away so suddenly? All I did was joke around with you a little in front of that confessional.
What was the joke?
Uh, okay... anyway, I know this fugitive's a tough one, so I brought reinforcements.
Giving up my chance to witness the Nativity in the process. Aren't you touched?
I never requested assistance from the Notarial Hall.
I know, I know. You hardly ever work with the other executors.
You think I volunteered for this? Special orders, jointly issued by all seven tribunals of the Curia.
Pretty shocked when I got the orders... for a moment there, I thought Arturia had convinced you to commit treason.
I have no such intention, and she has no such power.
I have completed my assessment and determined that any risk to the Curia can be eliminated. You and your men may stand down now, Executor Richele.
Are you defying the special orders, Federico?
You have no authority to refuse.
My orders were to oversee Federico's apprehension of Arturia before the Nativity, and to bring both of you in, should there be any suspicious behavior.
Not so fast, Senior Executor.
Were you talking to me, Seniora Arturia?
Of course.
I've been sitting in that monastery ever since I got back. I think I would like to stretch my legs for a while longer still.
It's been more than a decade since I last saw night fall upon the Holy City... the view from this spot is remarkable.
So you're resisting arrest... Oh, I do so hate overtime.
Stay behind us, Sister Hildegard.
You do the honors, Federico. You've been on this criminal's case for years. No one knows her tricks better than you.
Looks like the Lateran has long had its eye on you, Fede.
Raise your gun. It won't be the first time.
Arturia stands at the edge of the roof. Federico has many questions, but he can see that her fingers are already touching the strings.
Behind her, Federico sees the Shrine of Nurtury, so tall he cannot see the top, and the infinite crowd meandering towards it.
Richele and his other colleagues stand behind him. Federico hears the sound of weapons being locked and loaded.
Tension runs high. It is as though he must prove something as executor.
There is no time to think. Federico raises his gun.
You have admitted to escaping confinement, Arturia. By the authority of the Lateran Notarial Hall, I am hereby reassessing your danger level and apprehension procedures.
You must fulfill your obligations as a Lateran citizen and work with us to resolve any threat to Laterano.
Without warning, the muzzle of Federico's gun flashes.
At the same time, music pours out of the cello, becoming one with the hymns emitting from the direction of the Shrine of Nurtury, drowning out the sounds on the roof.
Ugh—!
Federico! What are you doing?!
I shot at the building beside you. I did not strike any of you.
But... that hurts... Mastigia! I think a rock hit my head... am I bleeding?
Do you know what you've done, Federico...?
Yes, you are bleeding.
I will submit a detailed report once the incident is resolved.
...I will include a detailed explanation of the head injury you have suffered.
That was decisive, Senior Executor.
Now, how do you intend to handle all these unconscious people... what are you looking for?
Richele has an annual pass for the confessionals. They can deliver these people to a hospital.
Now, share your discoveries and work with me to resolve any dangers to Laterano.
It's just a guess.
You chased me all the way here, then got your cohorts out of the way. You've evidently arrived at the same theory I have.
It's almost time, Fede.
Perhaps the city itself will soon tell us exactly what has happened to it.
I've never seen so many outsiders in Laterano.
That's for sure.
The last Summit of Nations didn't have enough guests to fill a single hotel.
Just about every cardinal in the Curia is here... it's so crowded. The air is even more awkward than the Basilica.
We're far enough on the edge, Mostima. We're like the cleaners standing against the wall of a casino.
Whatever this Nativity is, it's probably Andoain bringing out a Sankta kid who just learned to talk, so the Matriarch standing there gets to ceremonially polish the kid's halo...
All symbolic puff. We could be home watching One-Armed Chainsaw Guy 2 instead.
Let's just think of it as watching Nurturer Andoain work.
Haven't you already said all that needs to be said to him?
My heart's been feeling twitchy for some reason...
Do you feel it, Mostima?
Yeah.
Fiammetta turns sideways. She has never seen such a serious expression on her friend's face.
I'm feeling nervous... no, anxious, fearful. It's excitement and expectation, and I don't know why...
I can't describe it. It's like my heartbeat isn't my own.
Is this empathy?
You're a fallen Angel and I'm a Liberi. Neither of us has the power. No, it's not just us...
Fiammetta scans the crowd, and is now certain that this heartbeat is not an illusion.
The emotions that she is feeling are written on every face in the crowd. Every heart present throbs to this beat, which does not belong to them.
...Look up.
What?
Comfort bells.
Fiammetta follows her friend's gaze.
All the doors and windows were closed when the Nativity began. Not so much as a breeze can get in.

But the comfort bells are swaying in time, the ones hanging in the Hall of Enlightenment with them, and the ones in the corridors leading to the nurseries. Yet they do not produce the chimes that they should.
A sort of medium... like the musical devices that Leithanians use.
If my guess is right, we're feeling the heartbeat of the mother who is giving birth.
It's exactly the same!
It's exactly the same as what I felt when I waited outside the delivery room when my wife gave birth!
I could feel the unfathomable pain, but she would not give up. Her emotions tugged at mine, and we both spoke the name that we had already decided...
'Bailey, Bailey...'
Lord above!
The mother fainted? She's too weak. The pain of birthing is more than she can take... i-it's not good!
My heart's about to leap out of my mouth.
She's not giving up...
Lord, Holy City Most Pure, have mercy on us.
Have mercy on us.
Have mercy on us.
So... this really is a form of... 'empathy'.
Comfort bells sway violently throughout the hall. The ever-shifting emotions in the irregular heartbeats tug at everyone's nerves.
No one knows who the mother is, what her name is, how old she is, where she came from... but everyone knows what she is experiencing. No one moves a single step.

The fragile newborn has not yet triumphed over death. The struggle to break through the shell of ignorance seems eternal. Inside the Shrine of Nurtury, thousands of people experience a childbirth together—
Such clear empathy should never have existed in Laterano.
After a long, long time...
The heartbeat begins to weaken, until all is silent.
Connect the sensor wire, and activate the timer.
The last bomb... is ready!
Dammit, it's really hard not to march to this heartbeat!
*pant*—*pant*—
Fortunately, I've got the quickest brain in Penguin Logistics!
I could play Super Music Tycoon with one hand and raygun bottle shooting with the other, and beat both the boss and Texas at the same time!
All that practice wasn't for nothing!
Two minute countdown.
Blow up this corridor, and the path to the nursery will be cut off.
I hope my sister's intel is good...
The Shrine of Nurtury took a pregnant Sarkaz from a nomadic abbey, and got her to give birth to a Sarkaz at the Summit of Nations in this crazy ceremony...
I don't know what they're trying to do, but if I can keep her from appearing in front of the crowd—
Excuse me, you're in the way.
The mother is about to give birth. I want to get closer and pray for her.

Sorry, I'll…
Wait.
Pregnant... Sarkaz... aren't you the one?!
Yeah. What of it?
Huh?
Shouldn't you be giving birth—
Lemuel, right?
We've met before. Your sister brought you to our place.
Oh, er, Fiammetta's grandpa... salve...
The others are searching for the bombs and neutralizing them, Reverend Patrizion.
Good work.
Stop this Sankta. She has more explosives on her.
Snipers are in position. We will shoot to kill if the suspect attempts to escape.
Phew.
Reverend Patrizion...
You're late, Cardinal Lemuen.
Take the suspect back to the Seventh Tribunal.
But if the Nativity isn't about this Sarkaz, then...?
The heartbeat that resonated throughout the hall has quieted down.
A door opens to a corridor. Exusiai looks in the direction of the sound—
Carrying a small bundle, the Nurturer stands in the light shining through the door. His hands and robes are stained with blood, but the bundle is clean.
With head bowed, he walks slowly toward the Hall of Enlightenment, towards the waiting Matriarch. The crowd parts to let him through.
Thank you, Andoain.
The Matriarch takes the newborn from the Nurturer. She cannot see, and thus does not realize that his brow is furrowed in sorrow.
The Matriarch gently opens the bundle, and reveals the newborn—
A fledgling beast.
He leans against the Matriarch's shoulder, mucous membrane still sticking to its infantile paws. The wet and creased fur shows no sign of relaxing.
His eyes are closed, his breathing barely audible.
As you can see, the first life that the Shrine of Nurtury has brought into this world is this fledgling arborbeast.
His weight is nothing. His weight is everything.
Before I came to Laterano, I found his mother in an abandoned nomadic abbey. She was so famished, her feathers fallen, that I did not at first recognize her as an arborbeast.
A hunter's spear had impaled the mother's abdomen, but missed the fetus by inches.
I do not know how his mother hid for so long, how she survived, and how she stayed awake through the pain and hunger.
Until now.
I thank this strong mother, our Sankta and Liberi brethren, and our honored guests from afar—
This child was born amidst our prayers. He is a miracle that you have made together.
But why...?
Why do his eyes not open?
Matriarch. The newborn's breathing is growing weaker...
I tried everything I could think of. His mother gave her all.
Perhaps the mother's injuries were too grave, depriving the fetus of nutrition...
Andoain, we may all have forgotten one thing.
Do you mean...?
The childbirth was long and painful. But from beginning to end, all we felt through the comfort bells, through empathy, was a single heartbeat.
This child was so weak that he could not even express his desire to live, the most primal, most instinctive cry of life.
Andoain bows his head and does not speak.
Is this what you thought the Nativity would be, Mostima?
No.
Normally, we'd already be cutting the cake beside a crying baby...
Damn, this is depressing.
Do they mean that after all that effort from the mother, the baby was stillborn?
Che cazzo! How could this be?!
Lord above!
Is there anything we can do? Like what we did just now, the thousands of us here...
What if I never felt Bailey's heartbeat after hours of waiting outside the delivery room...? What would my wife and I have done...?
No, I can't bear to imagine it!
Poor baby, if you can hear my voice...
Somewhere in the crowd, someone begins sobbing.
Gradually, more and more people lose control of their emotions. Their cries join together and echo through the halls, for the poor mother and her stillborn child.
Then, another cry rings out.
It is not a sad sound. It is young and bright.

The fledgling arborbeast opens its eyes, and, as though sensing the emotions of the crowd, cries out for the first time in its life. It stretches its limbs, and the sheen miraculously returns to its fur.

The Matriarch embraces the newborn life tightly, crying tears of joy. One such tear drops from the empty eye sockets behind her veil, falling upon the fledgling's head, reflecting the light of the sun.
The fledgling does not stop crying. As though driven by the wailing, the light begins to break free of the void's shackles, becoming rich and full—
A halo.
The mark of the Sankta, the symbol of the Law, the embodiment of truth. It has illuminated this unfamiliar creature—
A shining, miraculous beacon.
The Lord has once again answered our prayer. He has blessed this child.
This is a miracle not seen in a thousand years. Its appearance on the eve of calamity is a revelation from the Law.
We are all witnesses. We are all participants.
Henceforth, we are born equal and one, beginning with the Holy City, and ending with the Holy City.
—As Sankta.
In the Matriarch's embrace, the newborn licks her arms. He has awakened.
Once again, the comfort bells sway. A new heartbeat, strong and regular, fills the Shrine of Nurtury.
Laterano granted a halo to an arborbeast fledgling?
Even the most incredible miracle in The Saints' Travels pales in comparison.
Is this a divine blessing, or a terrible calamity?
My greater concern is whether it is true, whether it should be believed.
Since when did you start doubting everything you see?
There are too many details, Arturia.
The unusual confessional.
The boundless wheat fields.
The crackdown on the manufacturing of firearms and explosives. The firearms workshops being shut down.
The sudden change in Landen Monastery's role.
The abrupt changes in the administration of the Curia. The colleagues who suddenly appeared to interfere with my investigation.
It was the appearance of Richele and his men, despite the fact that he should not have known my movements, that confirmed my suspicions. Otherwise, I would have continued my efforts to apprehend you.
All of these anomalies converge at this Shrine of Nurtury, which seems to have appeared out of nowhere, and the Nativity that so many have fixed their eyes on.
The missing mother you were investigating from the nomadic abbey was the pregnant arborbeast?
Yes.
By chance, my music crossed paths with the voice of her heart. I followed the trail here.
The singular item that brought me to my conclusion, of greater weight than any of the above, is how the Lateran people are seemingly oblivious to all of this.
Everything in life has been turned upside-down, but in this country, this city, life goes on, orderly as ever, as though things have always been so.
When six million Laterans continue to live as they always have, it's hard for us to convince ourselves that we're the only sane ones.
Self-doubt is an invaluable experience for a hardhead like you, isn't it?
Are you certain now?
Yes.
Mathematics, physics, geography, archaeology, astronomy... no theory or knowledge in science, theology, or philosophy can explain this.
A city with a population of six million, sharing the same hallucination.
But the present circumstances clearly run contrary to the order of Laterano and the foundation of the Law.
I have no doubt.
What a coincidence. Neither do I—

lines.st: The sound of comfort bells echoes in the sky above the city. The cello's music draws upon a crack, like a hand reaching into the city. The next instant, both sounds are brought to an abrupt halt.
Silence.
The crowds that come and go in front of the square have never existed. Laterano has never been so quiet, even when the city is gazed upon from the highest roof of the Basilica.
Federico and Arturia are unsure if they blinked. A scene most incomprehensible plays out before their eyes—
The shrine continues to tower over the city, utterly out of place, but now dream-like colors begin to run across its surface.
Almost reaching the clouds, it spills its colors upon the pristine white buildings surrounding it.
Doors and windows open to the streets and alleys of Laterano, wherever the shrine's shadow touches, and creatures the likes of which have never been seen before emerge from them...
The scene is indescribable, but there is no doubt in Federico or Arturia that this is the real Laterano.
The next instant, the same word slips from their mouths.
Calamity.

lines.x: Slow down! You got a death wish or something? We're carrying a damned Originium plate! We're all dead if you drop it!
Seriously, what the hell is wrong with this family, using this as a pickling stone? Do they like their pickles with a touch of Originium?
Shut your *Sarkaz expletive* mouth and keep moving.
We gotta clear this whole district before the end of the day, unless you want the overseer to chew our heads off after the Lich chews his.
So many houses, and we got to clean every single speck of Originium off 'em... doesn't Babel have a dedicated construction team? Why don't we just tear it all down and rebuild from scratch?
Say, it's gonna take some getting used to, not having any Originium around.
I said, shut your *Sarkaz expletive* mouth.
We didn't have a choice but to rely on the mines. Now that Kazdel doesn't need to worry about energy anymore, of course we gotta clean house...
Unless you LIKE living in a den of Originium—hey, watch where you're going, Lich!
Keep your threads tucked away, Lich. Who knows what might happen if they got tied up in the Originium...
That's not very polite.
You can cut the threads with your horns if they do get tied up. But who knows what dimension my thread will drag you into, if it comes to that?
Alright, I don't think I should scare you any further, gentlemen. Go ahead.
Did I imagine it? What's that light?
It came from the house up there, maybe? Let's take a look.
...I'll go.
Wha?
Those houses are right by the agricultural plate that has just been integrated. The light must be from one of the soil beacons, in reaction to the Originium plate that you're carrying.
If Wiš'adel finds out the farmland that she's spent two years cultivating got polluted...
Alright. Let's get outta here.
D-Don't come any closer!
Ugh...
Ermengarde looks at the agitated girl standing in front of her, trying to shield the man behind her with her tiny body.
It's a Sankta man, kneeling feebly on the ground. His halo and wings have shattered, their pieces still trembling and flickering, like dying fireflies.
The Sankta does not speak. It is as though he has fallen into a deep slumber.
Who are you? Are you here to take us away?
The girl is all tensed up. Ermengarde bends down and puts her hands on her knees, doing her best to show that she intends no harm, even wagging her energized tail friendly.
I mean no harm, young lady.
I sent the other Sarkaz away. Did you heard that? Maybe I can help you... help him.
Ermengarde closes the door behind her, shutting out any prying eyes.
She adjusts her hat. Invisible threads reach out through an unknowable dimension.
Master.
All that red-tape and long-winded tomes in the spires made me sick, but I should thank the twin sheep for their hospitality, compared to what I have to deal with now.
Every policy has to be communicated verbally, and you have to explain things over and over again. When are we going to implement basic education for government employees?
This is urgent.
...Go ahead.
I'm at the furnace node. I found two Sanktas with broken halos... one of them doesn't look right at all.
Bring them here. Try not to let anyone see you. We don't need a scene in the streets.
I'll go wake Wiš'adel. Looks like it's time for our Speaker to speak at an emergency meeting.
...The list is a long one.
You're saying that all forty-three Sanktas stationed at spires across Zwillingstürme have abandoned their posts without permission.
Yes, Ewigegnade.
They have already left Leithanian soil, forcing their way through the checkpoint at the Erdenherre border.
Leithanien took in these errari as an act of kindness. Now they have chosen desertion... without any warning.
Not entirely.
The night watch of the Reichsmusikhochschule witnessed a Sankta scholar leaving, and noted that his halo and wings were shattered, as if there was a... serious accident.
A Fallen?
The symptoms do not align exactly.
How do you think this should be dealt with?
A-Are you asking for my opinion?
Of course I am.
You are the mouth of the Austere Authority, Michael Wolfgang.
You are the only one who can speak with her voice in this realm, besides me.
Well...
You've grown in the past few years, both physically and mentally. Hildegard has taught you well.
I must commend you for making the report that you just did.
You have, in effect, admitted that you have access to certain... exceptional channels.
The Sanktas of Leithanien have fallen and deserted en masse. This is a serious matter... you are the only one capable of dealing with it, Ewigegnade.
I know that you, the Urtica boy, and even that Gräfchen are doing a few things under the surface. As a reward, I'm willing to turn a blind eye to that.
Now, back to the topic at hand.
The border guard reports that they seem to be headed for Laterano. It's likely that the Lateran Curia is involved.
Shall we send the Empresses' Voice to confront the Curia?
One year ago, after Das Kaiserinnensfest, Yvangelista XI communicated to me that he wishes to work hand-in-hand with Leithanien.
The anomalous circumstances with the Sanktas may indicate that the calamity the Pope had feared, so much that he would seek our help, has come at last.
Issue an edict, in the name of Eternal Grace and Austere Authority, to summon the best casters and scholars among all the spires.
Calamity will spare no corner of this great land. We must hasten our studies into the secret retrieved from the chaos.
Yes, Your Majesty.
I will arrange for it at once.
Perhaps not entirely at once.
Wh-What I mean is, I will instruct the Empresses' Voice to...
The eventide is beautiful tonight, Michael. Stay and enjoy it with me.
The wound in the sky has healed. She would have appreciated this too... would she not?
Tsk...
...East of the east of La Unidad, Laterano, holy city of white♪
The guns there shoot fireworks, the targets all apples♪
When we pray sincere, He gives us bread♪
When we seek salvation, He sets us free♪
Sing, sing until you're asleep.
The god in your dreams is kinder than Laterano's.
You didn't need to go this far on the night watch, Aurela.
...Get lost.
This is the Minoan border. Those Singas nobles won't go any further. You're safe.
The abbey is no more. We go our separate ways.
Ten years ago, you came to my abbey and protected my plate from those nobles without a word. Now, you're leaving without a word.
You preach your teachings, I protected the kids in exchange for a new roof over my head, that's all.
Don't think too much of yourself.
You can't leave, Aurela.
I'm not asking.
Our destination is the same. We'll go with you to Laterano.
And when did I say I was bringing you with me?
We've been together for ten years. It's a long time for a K'uk'ulkan.
Maybe I don't trust Laterano that much. But I do trust you.
The Lateran stripped you of citizenship, and you've cursed the city many times while I sang the hymns...
But look at yourself. After all these years in Bolívar, you still won't take off the gun-knight's armor...
Even though you've fallen, lost the ability to wield a patron firearm, you somehow got your hands on this Blacksteel gun, which means you never—
Shut up.
The fallen angel suddenly raises her gun, the muzzle almost reaching the K'uk'ulkan's lips.
If you trust me, you should remember that I've told you many times the Holy City is not so friendly, kind, or tolerant as you imagine...
Half the people who followed you from the abbey are not Sankta.
There are plenty of places you can go after leaving Bolívar.
Perhaps what we follow is not the true Lateran faith, but it's what kept a modicum of order in our lives amidst the chaos of Bolívar.
There's no way I can convince the others to go somewhere else. They want to make the pilgrimage.
Nor can I convince myself. Laterano is part of us now, after these long years.
You've got to be kidding.
Think of the Sanktas in the abbey. They've never known Laterano, only the trauma of the Fall.
Only by returning to Laterano will they know what happened. Only then will the fear be driven out. Only then can their 'sickness' be cured.
Let us walk together for a little while longer, Aurela.
I'm not going back to Laterano because I miss home.
Ten years ago, an old man said something like this to me, at another abbey. He was the one who exiled me in the name of Laterano, because of some silly mistake.
He wanted to hear me repent, hear me pray. He said there was a chance I could go back, if I truly, sincerely repented for my sins...
You refused?
I sure did.
Something seems to have gone really wrong in the Holy City. I'm going to seize this chance for revenge.
Nueda steps forward, and presses her face against the cold gun barrel.
We won't stop you.
We just need a guide. Someone who knows the way.
Someone like you.
The hymns begin to die down, replaced by the sound of children crying out.
Aurela lets out a laugh and puts away her gun.
The Shrine of Nurtury smears its colors on the surrounding buildings, opening numerous portals through which strange creatures with halos emerge one-by-one, falling to the ground.
Did those things get the blessing of Laterano too? It wasn't just the baby arborbeast?
(Claps excitedly)
(Sways uncertainly)
They crawl on the ground, wagging their heads furiously, still trying to adjust to their halos. They bellow in a low, rhythmic voice, as if joining together in prayer.
Urgh. That's one ugly, chaotic voice of the heart.
If everyone in the Holy City has fallen into a sort of mass hallucination of an unknown source...
Does this scene before us mean that the boundary between reality and falsehood is being blurred, and that Laterano is being invaded by a material threat?
This is even more incomprehensible than what we experienced in the Pavillon.
One of them has noticed us, Fede.
(Praying)
The noise we made seems to have disrupted their ritual.
An enlightened creature turns toward Federico and Arturia, perhaps because it missed part of the prayer.
Its halo flashes, and its bloated hide seems to grow light and lithe, approaching the duo at high speed.
Federico raises his gun, but the halo-adorned creature pays no heed, not even stopping its prayer.
Fall back. The nature of these creatures is yet unknown. We must not be surrounded.
I thought you would shoot without hesitation.
Are these creatures our kin, Fede? Are you in violation of the Law if you shoot?
Is that a joke, or simply an ill-timed curiosity?
Your patron firearm is lost, and I can neutralize these creatures without discharging mine.
We will determine whether they should be considered our kindred, but not now.
Perhaps there is someone who can answer our questions.
...Pope Yvangelista XI.
Move!
Such a sound... do not fear, child.
Matriarch.
You too, Nurturer. Thank you for your work.
I simply did what I could.
The child is still weak. Take him back to his mother, Andoain.
I will come and find you.
You seem unwell, Matriarch. Should I escort you to your apartments?
I am fine. The heartbeat continues to echo within me.
Not just the mother. I can hear countless hearts resonating, so powerful it seems they could bring down this shrine.
The people have begun to link with one another, irrespective of race or background... but some have rejected the future we revealed.
The Nativity has come to an end. Our kindred need not stay here. We should prepare for the next stage.
Understood. You should retire. I will take care of the rest.
Once again, welcome to the Shrine of Nurtury.
Whether you understood the Matriarch's proposal at the summit, and regardless of what you think of it, the birth and enlightenment of this arborbeast is undoubtedly a clear revelation.
On how to remove the barriers between nations and races... on how to resolve hatred and build lasting peace... on facing the calamity that hangs over our heads together.
Beginning with the Nativity, the second Summit of Nations now enters a new stage—
Laterano will share our halo with all races!
'May our Lord share the blessings with all.'
Fiammetta! Mostima! Over here!
Lemuel?
When did you come back? Why didn't you tell us?
Nice outfit, huh? Including those handcuffs...
I wanted to surprise you. Well... hee hee.
(Scratches head)
What happened, Lemuen?
...It's a long story.
I could buy you two minutes, if you want to make a break for it.

Sure—
On second thought, maybe next time. I've caused enough trouble for my sister already.
Did I interrupt a reunion?
Velliv.
Can you take the suspect to the Seventh Tribunal on your own, Cardinal Lemuen?
Or do you need support? After all, attempted sabotage of the Shrine of Nurtury and Summit of Nations is a serious charge.
If you're concerned, you are free to come with us, or have a gun-knight keep watch.
Don't talk like that. After all, according to your conclavist Cardinal Aide Amos's testimony, you picked up Lemuel's trail at another scene beforehand...
Are you saying I tried to cover for my sister?
Well, not exactly. All the evidence is circumstantial, and there is nothing that proves you did anything wrong.
Do you recall our previous conversation in the Shrine?
I admire your ability and resolve greatly, Lemuen the Silent. But I also know when these virtues of yours are most prominently displayed.
Besides, it's natural for us to be cautious when your sister is involved. I hope you understand.
No need to worry. Letting El run around with all these charges against her won't help her.
Of course. I'm sure the misunderstanding can be resolved, as long as Lemuel cooperates.
One moment, Shimmering Vigil... and Legatus Mostima.
What?
Please remain here at the Shrine of Nurtury. The Curia has a mission for you.
They... took El away?
After all the effort it took for Magister to get me off the hook...
Come on, Vani... isn't there anything else you can do?
Don't bother knocking, Andoain. I have nothing to hide from you.
The mother?
Sleeping.
I brought the child. His feathers are not yet grown in, but he is surprisingly lively. I thought perhaps you might want to pet him again.
Thank you. It is comforting to me.
This place...
Do you remember? We built it to house the mother when we came to Laterano. It became the first nursery of the shrine.
I remember. We made this simple yet warm bed together, and hung the comfort bells that gave her restful sleep.
The room is no longer in use, but I come back from time to time, if only for prayer.
This was our starting point, yours and mine.
(Stretches happily)
I sense that he likes this place too.
His halo is so bright. I noticed it the moment you brought him here.
It is the miracle that you have long believed in.
But I don't understand. What brought on the premonitions of the miracle when you first encountered the mother?
I had no premonitions, Andoain, I only saw your struggling heart.
She was on her last legs, and what she needed was not healing, but her last rites. Yet you wanted to save her, and you would not allow the idea to be dispelled by common sense.
I believed that this thought, which you protected so dearly, could create the miracle of life.
Life is a wonderful thing, one that can never be measured by the confines of common sense.
Anyone would want to save her, Matriarch.
Therefore, anyone can create miracles. Miracles are so because people do not believe in the power of faith.
Is it the halo that gives Sankta faith, or faith that gives Sankta the halo? Have you ever pondered it?
Our Law, empathy, and firearms, and the Paradise that we built atop them...
Did they all arise from a blessing without a cause, as common sense would indicate?
...I don't know.
The past has its beginnings, as does the future.
The Shrine of Nurtury... This towering edifice was not built in a day. It began as a simple, ordinary nursery.
Our nursery...
Did we start with the blueprints of such an imposing shrine? No. We merely wished for a safe, comfortable room for that mother...
And yet, its frenzied evolution led to what it is now.
That is how a future where all are saved can sprout from our faith.
Matriarch... I followed you because I had long searched for such a future, and you happened to know the way.
But I never thought, nor could I comprehend, why you believed it could begin with me.
What I'm able to do is, ultimately, insignificant.

Insignificant trifles and grandiose feats are but a hair's breadth from one another. You have labored for many years over this grueling journey of yours. You should not let such a meaningless difference stay your steps.
I will need you to lift your spirits, for aside from caring for this mother and child, there are more deeds that must be done... such as the next Nativity.
You're early.
I'm already packed. You can just stick the seals on top so I can go back to bed.
Patrizion?
Hmph. I thought you were him.
I was told the last part of the handover would be handled by the Notarial Hall. Why did they send gun-knights?
That was assuming nothing had been tampered with.
What are you talking about?
Both the storage room and equipment show signs of recent use.
Cardinal Aide Amos must have made it clear when he last came here—
All gunsmithing operations were to cease immediately upon signing the papers. Any unauthorized work on patron firearms or explosives would be a violation of law.
I've had this little workshop for decades. I couldn't let it go so easily...
I just wanted to hear these old boys move again one last time and shed some tears over it. Is that so wrong?
You've already said as much.
Your apprentice, Vannini, the one you took with you from the Shrine of Nurtury—where is she?
Must've gone back to watch the Nativity.
She's not a kid anymore. Besides, I don't see so well. Can't keep an eye on her all the time.
In that case, let us continue this conversation somewhere else.
What if I don't want to?
I do not wish to resort to force, unless absolutely necessary.
It was Patrizion who sent you here, wasn't it?
Come with me.
S-Sister Hildegard, Reverend Gun-Knight?
Is she not in the monastery?
Y-Yes, but... she just had an accident. She's resting now.
What?
It's hard to explain...
A mobile confessional suddenly showed up at the monastery yesterday. Sister Hildegard was lying inside, unconscious...
There was no one else; someone had set the confessional's navigation system to bring her here. We're not sure what happened to her.
Perhaps we can have her report to you once she wakes up.
This is urgent. Bring me to her.
But...
*cough* *cough*... It's alright, Sister Gerain.
Hildegard! You're awake? The porridge...
I'm fine, just a little dizzy.
I ate the porridge on the table. I'm coughing because some of it went down the wrong pipe.
Reverend Gun-Knight, you came for Arturia, didn't you?
Yes, it's true that Arturia left Landen Monastery without permission...
But I went after her as soon as I found out! And I caught her! But something unexpected happened...
Please, give Landen Monastery a little more time... no, give me a little more time.
We would like you to come with us, Sister Hildegard.
Well... alright.
It would be best if you came too, Sister.
Me?
Sister Gerain doesn't have anything to do with this.
I was the one in charge of supervising Arturia. This is my fault...
No need to worry, Sister Hildegard.
With a major incident like this, the Curia needs as much information as we can get.
Now, follow me, please.
It is the greatest graduation ceremony in the history of St. Theodore's Comprehensive School... no, the greatest ever held in the entire history of Lateran education!

Seventeen giant fireworks burst up into the air as the seventeen-year old red-haired Sankta strums her strings wildly, opening her solo concert. The sound echoes through the campus, rattling the windows of nearby school buildings.
The pews near the stage, statues, and pool... apple pies of all kinds lie everywhere as gifts for her audience.
But the entire square is empty as the red-haired Sankta plays her guitar and sings, with no teachers or students to cheer her on.

It is no rare sight to see a student planning her own graduation ceremony in Laterano, but she is not even at the age to do so. She fails to get her permission to graduate early, even if she has already completed her preparatory courses for university.
But what a sin, to complete them without a ceremonial flair!
Students open their classroom windows and watch from afar as the red-haired Sankta strums and sings without a care, until her final number. Then, they witness an unforgettable sight—
The red-haired Sankta plays her last notes, bows, and thanks the air. The school gates then burst open, as more than a hundred automated confessionals flood into the campus.
Where the audience should have been are now confessionals with their doors open, all their 'mobile electronic confessors' joining the red-haired Sankta's performance with their volume set to maximum.
Neither Laterano's largest choir nor its noisiest heavy metal band could rouse such a racket!
Finally, the Pontifica Cohors rush to restore order on campus. As they persuade the confessionals to return to their routes, however, the instigator has long vanished from the scene...
Leaving only a single banner, unfolding as the last firework goes off—
'Let's send Lemuel off on her next adventure with a bang!'
Lemuel stops over at the Lateran port.
Hah, so there's a saint statue here too!
Weird how I never noticed before... They're actually pretty nice to look at!
This is her first time leaving Laterano for a place without halos, in her search for people and answers.
She suddenly thinks, for no particular reason, of how she might not return for a long, long time.
Didn't think they'd actually let you interrogate me, sis.
...Only because the previous seven cardinals got nothing out of you.
Come, El. Help me open this.

Why's this thing wrapped so tight—
Wow, a mille-feuille fruit tart?
The Seventh Tribunal is sure nice to suspects!
Come on, I made it myself. There's also this.
...Take it.
What's that now?
Your diploma.
After I awakened, the head of St. Theodore's—who was also mentoring me at the time—came to see me, and asked me to keep it for you.
Your application to graduate was rejected, but that ceremony you put together did indeed prove your skills.
After all, how could you get that many confessionals to perform with you, if you weren't really familiar with their programming and the intercity net's defense patterns?
So the school consulted the Curia, and granted you a diploma in the end.
Congratulations, El... even if it's a few years late.
Hehe. Thanks, sis.
I'm sure my letters found their way to you?
Uh... yeah.
You know, I made a bet with Mamma and Tata. They said you'd major in intercity net security and become an expert in the Notarial Hall.
But I thought you'd never waste your creative mind like that. I was sure you'd major in faith assistive engineering and design a new mobile confessional that'd shock the city.
In the end, you left Laterano, just like that.
Let me be clear, sis. I don't regret it one bit.
You know I'm not the type, and I'm even less the type to let anyone hold any regrets for me.
Which is why you rushed all your preparatory courses and threw that wild graduation ceremony, right?
Heheh.
I peppered Mostima with questions about you. Your work in Lungmen, your boss, your coworkers...
I know you went to Siracusa, to Sargon, and many more places besides.
You deal with all sorts of people every day. Gangs, cops, famiglie, lords ameer... you're always on the move...
Why does my life sound so different after going through a Mostima filter?
Well, I did live a nice life after leaving Laterano. Wasn't sad or anything.
I mean, you were still bedridden then. Didn't know how you got hurt, if you'd ever wake up, and Mostima refused to say a word.
I had to know what the heck happened!
You're my sister, you know?!
But that woman, bless her heart, kept avoiding me. I got a whole load of nothing from her.
Don't blame Mostima.
You know, sis. I've been through a lot these past few years.
There's some stuff I would have never thought about, if I stayed here.
In Lungmen, Mostima was the only Sankta around, but she lost her empathy.
Texas, Sora, Croissant, Bison, and the boss... At first, I made friends with them the same way I did here.
We're all besties now, but without empathy I sometimes still can't help but worry about silly little things...
I've been trying hard to overcome that, and every time I do, our ties become a little stronger.
But here, things are different. Like right now, I don't even have to think anything to know you wanna go somewhere else to give me a big proper hug.
What kind of collective has empathy turned us into?
Does it make us more, or less sensitive to others?
Does it make us so insensitive that it's easier to accept totally outrageous change?
Does this have something to do with everything you've done since you came back?

Laterano's sick, sis, just like you were back then—
We gotta wake it UP!
El, you need to give me more than that.
There's Pontifica Cohors guardsmen right behind these doors, right? I heard them when I came into the room.
Let me guess, are you going to say 'this is your last chance...' or something like that?
If you insist on refusing to explain why you disrupted the Summit of Nations and reveal the forces backing you, the Seventh Tribunal will have to hand you over to someone else.
I don't know what kind of punishment you'll face, El.
Laterano's leniency has never applied to those who violate the Law, especially these days—
C'mon—'Apple pie with mayonnaise!'
What?
Wh-What's going on? Was that an explosion downstairs?
A terror attack?
Aren't they interrogating the terrorism suspect upstairs right now? Who else would dare to attack the Seventh Tribunal?
Automated Confessional, 'ELCARO-001', in position!
A-A mobile confessional?
Fire.
Gah, what's going on? Who let that in here? Did they not realize this one's malfunctioning?
But that's just a mobile confessional, buddy.
Gah, who thought it'd be a good idea to put so many guns on a confessional?
Alert! A mobile confessional is attacking us and attempting to rescue the suspect Lemuel.
Calling all combat units, we need backup!
I repeat!
A-Again!
Now the left staircase... blow it up and I can slow them down for a little longer...
Do you read me, El?! I'm here to save you!
Vani?
Haha! Sick idea! I never expected you to find out the secret of this confessional and use it to help me out!
I-It's nothing. It took me so long to finally realize there is something unusual with its plate number. I noticed it when you stopped it before, but I didn't think too much.
All confessionals start with 'LC', for 'Laterano automated mobile Confessional', but this one starts with 'ELC'...
She practically scanned the entire city, stopping confessionals one by one... until I noticed her.
Alright, Exusiai. Pontifica Cohors reinforcements will be here soon, and the Nurtury guards are also on the move. We need to get out now.
Coming right up!
The guards respond quickly, constructing an emergency passage upstairs, assembling to face the chaos.

Exusiai breaks the door open as the confessional speeds up toward her, blocking bullets from the Pontifica Cohors. She hops onto the vehicle, and steadies herself as the confessional wobbles wildly from her landing.
The Sankta's red hair flutters between the bullets and gunsmoke.
Confessor... uh, wait, you've been exposed, so we can go back to normal, right?
Leader, savior, Doctor! You're! So! Awesome!

opts: There's six guns on the front outer rack.
There's three guns in here.
Feel free to use them as you please.
Do what you do best.
Vannini switched out the bullets for non-lethal ones.
